data.title: Dutch Bros: Visibility To EBITDA Growth Has Improved (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Dutch Bros Inc. (NYSE:BROS) previously with a hold rating, as the valuation was already pricing in the upsides even though I like the fundamentals. The story has meaningfully improved since my last write-up. Core catalysts I highlighted, such as digital adoption, food innovation, and stronger marketing, are now delivering tangible results, driving a clear inflection in traffic and transactions. With valuation now more reasonable and visibility to EBITDA growth improving, I believe the setup is compelling enough to upgrade my rating.

The Growth Flywheel Is Gaining Momentum

Apart from valuation coming down (more below), a key reason for my upgrading to buy is because the drivers I previously identified as catalysts (digital adoption and food innovation) are now delivering tangible results. This can be seen in the latest quarterly results that showed a clear inflection point in traffic and transactions. For better reference, I will draw some data from the latest quarterly results: System-wide same-shop sales [SSS] grew 6.1%, supported by a 3.7% lift in transactions, in which company-operated shops' SSS grew 7.8%, supported by a 5.9% increase in traffic. The focus on traffic and transaction datapoints matters because it tells us that growth is being driven by bringing more customers through the door and increasing visit frequency, not just leaning on price, which is far healthier and more sustainable.

On digital adoption, I believe this has contributed significantly to the momentum. For instance, the Mobile Order & Pay [MOP] platform, which I previously highlighted at 11% penetration, has moved higher to 11.5% of transactions. While the 50 bps may not seem like much, the impact is huge because it is successfully changing consumer behavior by pushing demand to the underutilized walk-up window, which now accounts for ~15% of orders versus 10% previously (2Q25 earnings call). This improvement in throughput and service speed, especially during the morning daypart, provides tangible proof that BROS's digital strategy is driving operational efficiency while enhancing customer experience.

As for the food innovation initiative. What began as a limited test has now doubled in scope to 64 stores and is delivering tangible results. The fact that management expanded the scope tells us that the strategy is working, and indeed, the streamlined eight-item menu has helped to lift both ticket and transaction counts, with management confirming that service times remain largely unaffected. In other words, BROS is able to drive up SSS while maintaining operational efficiency. This effectively de-risks the planned 2026 nationwide rollout, adding another clear driver for future SSS growth.

On another note, I also like to point out that BROS marketing execution has worked out really well. The decision to focus more on paid advertising has helped to drive higher brand awareness and traffic, which is particularly important in growth markets like Texas, where BROS needs to build awareness. The next leg to further drive up awareness is BROS plan to partner with consumer packaged goods [CPG] brands. The way this works is through a licensing partnership with a major manufacturer, for which BROS will roll out branded K-Cups and ground coffee to >50,000 retail outlets in 2026. Per management, this initiative is capital-light and margin-accretive, and if done well, it should serve as a powerful brand marketing tool that helps BROS to extend awareness well beyond its existing store footprint.

Put together, the combination of digital adoption, food innovation, effective marketing, and the upcoming CPG launch are solid growth drivers for SSS.

Valuation

On valuation, relative to June, when the stock traded at 40x forward EBITDA, multiples are now much more palatable at 29x. I am not shifting my P&L assumptions, as what I expect from BROS (in terms of catalyst) is playing out nicely. If anything, I think BROS's visibility to EBITDA growth (driven by revenue growth and margin expansion) has gotten higher. The one thing I have changed is to roll forward my estimates to value on FY27 numbers instead of FY26 (since we are approaching the end of this year). Assuming the same revenue growth and no margin expansion (to be conservative), I get to BROS reporting $395 million in adj. EBITDA. Adjusting for net debt, and you get to a market cap of $13 billion, or a share price of $80. For note, $80 is where BROS traded in February, so it is not far off the historical trading range. This also implies an upside of 34%.

Risk

While I have turned bullish, I think it is also fair to touch more on risk for BROS. While the mid-to-long-term bull case has become clearer, there are near-term issues that readers need to be aware of, which could result in stock price volatility. The first and more concerning one is the tougher comparisons as BROS goes into 2H25. Recall last year that BROS benefited from an outsized growth from the digital rollouts and elevated marketing in late 2024. This has set a high bar, and as those anniversaries come into play, y/y growth rates will likely moderate. The slower headlines may weigh on stock sentiment.

The next issue is on the cost front, in which management has already noted pressure building into 2H25. Beverage, food, and packaging costs are expected to be ~26% of company-operated sales, which means higher coffee prices have yet to flow into the P&L. This is an important detail, as it means gross margin headwinds are still to come, not behind us. Add to this the risk of tariffs on imported goods, which management explicitly called out, and the margin trajectory for 2026 becomes more uncertain. While BROS has demonstrated discipline in scaling sales and maintaining operational efficiency to protect margins, these cost issues are material risks, in my view. Depending on when it hits and how much it hits the P&L, it could temper the stock sentiment as well.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for BROS. The growth flywheel is working well across multiple fronts (digital, food, marketing, and soon CPG), which should drive SSS growth and margin expansion over the mid-to-longer term. With valuation now reset to a far more attractive level and long-term earnings visibility improving, I see a favorable risk/reward profile with ~34% upside potential.

data.title: Ares Capital: Don't Be Fooled By The Dividend Cut Naysayers (Upgrade)
data.description:
Ares Capital: My Caution On The BDC Leader Panned Out As Expected

I hope investors in Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) managed to avoid buying at the recent July highs, as the stock of the leading BDC topped out, heading into its Q2 earnings release then. And it isn't like the company didn't unveil what I thought was still a pretty decent report. If you recall, Ares Capital highlighted higher investment activity in Q2, while also maintaining a relatively low non-accruals ratio (1.2% in fair value terms). Moreover, it also highlighted spillover income that it could depend on to help sustain its dividend allocation in 2025, potentially lowering the market's worries about having to cut its prized dividends.

Despite that, I don't think the market can simply ignore the rate reduction headwinds heading into 2026, particularly as the Fed has finally executed its first interest rate cut for 2025. I held these concerns in my last ARCC write-up, cautioning investors from engaging with the stock too soon, as the risk/reward setup had worsened materially. Hence, I believe my downgrade has proved to be timely, as ARCC has markedly underperformed the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) since then, despite still trading at a relative discount to the broad market.

With the stock having already declined this week, falling to lows last seen in May, I think the market has astutely positioned for a more dovish Fed over the next 12 months. Given the pullback observed in ARCC while the market continues to print new highs, I think we can generally concur that investors don't think the premium observed in the stock is sustainable, as the current interest cycle has definitely peaked and is expected to turn even lower.

Ares Capital: Dealing With Lower Earnings. Dividend Cut Imminent?

Even analysts have penciled in a more challenging prognosis for its earnings capabilities over these two years, notwithstanding the company's best efforts in demonstrating its capital allocation discipline (during the higher interest rate period over the past two years). Yet, I also think it's pivotal to point out that management has been steadfast in telegraphing its confidence that ARCC could maintain its dividend payout, underscoring its 16-year track record without a cut.

Based on the revised Wall Street estimates, analysts expect core EPS of $1.89 to come in below the full dividend payouts of $1.92 for the year, suggesting there could be a need to rely on the spillover income ($878 million, amounting to $1.29 per share), while also being bolstered by the net realized gains of $117 million to help tank any unanticipated decline. Nevertheless, with investment activity ramping up toward the second half of 2025, I believe it should position the company well moving forward, notwithstanding the near-term challenges impeding buying sentiments.

Which is why I think the stock's decline (down 0.33%) was relatively muted following the Fed's interest rate decision yesterday. Based on the updated summary of economic projections, the FOMC's revisions point to two more rate cuts for 2025, and one more for 2026, and 2027, respectively. However, amidst the dovish posture, Fed Chair Powell also took the opportunity to remind us that future decisions will still be data-dependent, and by no means guaranteed.

Given the lack of an imminent economic recession, coupled with Ares Capital's robust backlog as of Q2 (totaling about $2.6 billion), I don't expect the market to go pessimistic on ARCC through 2026. Note that Ares Capital also retains substantive flexibility for ATM equity issuance, while also being bolstered by a relatively solid balance sheet (net leverage ratio of 0.98x and $6.5 billion in liquidity).

Moreover, lower policy rate tailwinds might also help boost originations markedly through 2026, making up for the lower base rates, while also underpinning low accrual rates. Therefore, I believe the astute capital allocation decisions taken when ARCC was then "over-earning," so to speak, should help defend the stock against a sharper selloff through next year.

Buy Or Sell ARCC Stock?

With the decline having panned out since its early 2025 top, ARCC's premium over its financial median has also evaporated. Accordingly, its forward core EPS multiple of 10.6x has dropped below the financial median of 11.2x, while its forward dividend yield of 9.1% has also closed the gap against its 10Y average of 9.5%.

If anything, I think Ares Capital investors thinking about doubling down or increasing exposure might want to take this time to contemplate whether the opportunity to do so is timely.

Based on ARCC's price action assessment, it's evident to me that the stock's uptrend bias is still well-anchored. The Fed's dovish turn has definitely impacted the market's confidence in its capital allocation strategy, but it hasn't been enough to reverse the bullish narrative fully.

And, with the premium in ARCC having already dropped markedly, I think the time to turn more constructive to a healthier 2026 (potentially higher originations and lower default risks) is opportune. Sans an economic recession (which isn't my base case), BDC investors biding their time on the industry leader should find the recent pullback appealing.

Rating: Upgrade to "Buy."

data.title: Blended Finance: Bridging The Sustainability Funding Gap
data.description:
By Patrick O'Connell, CFA and Marie Clara Buellingen

Blended finance has the potential to transform overlooked markets into investable opportunities.

The United Nations (UN) warns of a roughly US$4 trillion annual shortfall in financing for its sustainable development goals—a gap too large for the public sector to fill alone. Blended finance, which combines public, philanthropic and private capital, can help bridge this divide and unlock progress on global priorities such as poverty reduction, climate action and access to clean energy.

A decade after the UN adopted its 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, progress toward many of its goals remains stalled. While some elements of the plan, such as rural electrification, have advanced, insufficient funding has impeded others. Nearly 3.5 billion people live in countries where governments spend more on interest payments than on healthcare or education—leaving little capacity to invest in sustainable development.

At the same time, according to the IMF, global environmental, social and governance (ESG) funds have largely avoided emerging markets. Even though these economies drove the bulk of global GDP growth over the past 10 years, they were allocated only about 6% of global ESG portfolios (Display).

The Mismatch Between Investment Capital and Emerging Economies

Historical and current analyses do not guarantee future results.

*Of the US$489 trillion in assets held within EM economies excluding China

Left display as of December 31, 2022; right display as of April 30, 2025

Source: Perrine Toledano et al., From Promise to Performance: Reforming Blended Finance for Scale, Columbia Center on Sustainable Investment, June 2025; Deepali Gautam et al., Unlocking

Adaptation Finance in Emerging Market and Developing Economies, International Monetary Fund, November 19, 2024

This mismatch highlights a significant untapped opportunity. Blended finance offers a way to gather capital at scale, mobilizing both mainstream investors and those seeking impact. And it charts a path forward: expanding access to underserved markets and growth sectors while offering attractive potential risk-adjusted returns, typically in investment-grade packages.

But first, a quick word on taxonomy.

The Building Blocks of Blended Finance

Blended finance rests on three types of capital: public, philanthropic and private.

  • Public capital—such as development finance institutions, multilateral banks and government agencies—is usually the first mover. It provides commitments such as guarantees, subsidized loans, anchor investments and policy supports that reduce perceived risk for others.
  • Philanthropic capital—from foundations, donor funds or high-net-worth individuals—is often used to provide early grants, technical assistance or risk-absorbing funds that bridge gaps and unlock innovation. In some frameworks, it’s bundled with concessional forms of public finance under the label “catalytic” or “concessionary” capital. Here, concessional simply means that capital is provided on below-market terms. (In this article, we use “catalytic capital” to mean concessional public or philanthropic funding that accepts below-market economics or first-loss positions to de-risk projects so that private capital can provide the scale.)
  • Private capital—including institutional investors, banks and corporations—typically comes later, bringing scale and efficiency, along with an expectation of market-level returns. In some schematics, this is called “commercial” or “market-rate” capital.

Each of these building blocks plays a distinct role: public capital lays the foundation, philanthropic capital helps further de-risk projects and attract private capital, and private capital drives growth and market depth (Display). Combined in complementary ways, they can mobilize far more resources than any source could on its own.

The Capital Mix That Makes Blended Finance Work

For illustrative purposes only

As of July 31, 2025

Source: AllianceBernstein (AB)

Growing the Ecosystem: Coordinating Across the Capital Stack

Mobilizing capital at the scale required to close a US$4 trillion gap is no small task. Developing nations must secure financing on manageable terms that can also attract investors—requiring coordination among asset managers, development banks, sovereign governments, rating agencies, philanthropic foundations, insurers and nongovernmental organizations.

Attracting large, durable flows of private capital is especially difficult. Early co-creation among these stakeholders can help produce transparent structures that can be replicated. And catalytic resources brought into the riskiest stages of development can help turn early concepts into viable investment opportunities—building a stronger pipeline for private capital to follow.

A major step toward that kind of coordination came with the inaugural Impact and Blended Finance Conference, held earlier this year. Co-hosted by AllianceBernstein, Societe Generale and the Emerging Markets Investors Alliance, the event brought together stakeholders from across the capital stack and served as a model of buy- and sell-side partnership.

Growing the Ecosystem: Creating a Repository

At the conference, Boston Consulting Group and Societe Generale proposed creating a centralized repository to support blended-finance transactions. By aggregating data on funding availability by region, sector and instrument type, such a resource could streamline dealmaking, reduce transaction costs and increase transparency.

A centralized repository could be a major catalyst for expanding blended finance, helping investors deploy capital more efficiently and at greater scale. It could also make catalytic funding easier to access by reducing variability in eligibility, application and reporting requirements that too often slow projects down.

Growing the Ecosystem: Measuring Success

Scaling blended finance requires clear and consistent measures of success. Investors need to understand both the financial outcomes and the environmental or social impact of each transaction. Developing a common set of standards facilitates reliability and comparability. Such a rigorous approach helps reduce the risk of greenwashing—ensuring that capital is flowing to projects with demonstrable impact while also meeting investor economics. Over time, this kind of transparency builds confidence and encourages more investors to participate.

While stronger coordination, a centralized repository and clearer impact metrics are essential for scaling blended finance tomorrow, investors can already find tangible structures they can put capital into today.

Novel Tools for Sustainable Investment

Among the most visible applications of blended finance are innovative bond structures that channel capital directly into sustainability projects. Two examples—debt-for-nature swaps and outcome bonds—show how creative approaches can deliver both measurable impact and competitive returns.

Debt-for-nature swaps allow developing countries to reduce sovereign debt in exchange for investments in conservation. The structures are complex and require coordination among asset managers, multilateral development banks, sovereign governments and conservation organizations. But the benefits are threefold: for countries at risk of default, these arrangements are a cost-effective way of easing debt burdens; proceeds are directed toward critical projects such as protecting rainforests or safeguarding endangered species; and investors may purchase the bonds at attractive valuations.

One recent debt-for-nature swap not only helped conserve 60,000 square kilometers of Ecuadorian marine territory but also delivered yields higher than many US corporate bonds with comparable credit ratings.

Outcome bonds, like debt-for-nature swaps, fund sustainable development but often with more clearly defined goals. These bonds typically appeal to investors focused on a specific theme, such as restoring the population of black rhinos or reforesting the Amazon.

In some cases, coupon payments are adjusted based on project results—providing investors with both measurable impact and principal protection when issued or guaranteed by a highly rated development institution such as the World Bank.

Historically, outcome bonds have had higher yields than other assets of similar credit quality, and their impact is quantifiable: investors know precisely how many trees were planted, how much carbon was sequestered, or how many rhinos were conserved.

Turning Potential into Outcomes

For blended finance to achieve its promise, it must deliver both measurable impact and returns attractive enough to draw mainstream investors.

Catalytic capital plays a critical role in unlocking larger flows of private investment. When catalytic resources de-risk transactions, sustainability-linked instruments can advance global development priorities while also offering compelling returns to investors.

The challenge is vast, but so is the opportunity: blended finance gives investors a chance to participate at the start of a market poised for major growth.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia And Intel: Huge Deal, Soaring Price
data.description:
Article Thesis

On Thursday morning, Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) and Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) announced a deal that will see the two companies work together in chip development. Nvidia will also take a stake in Intel, which can be interpreted as a vote of confidence and which should strengthen INTC's hurting balance sheet. This deal could, I believe, be beneficial for both parties, but especially Intel should benefit, which is why the positive share price reaction makes a lot of sense.

My Past Coverage On Intel And Nvidia

I have written about both Intel and Nvidia here on Seeking Alpha in the past. My most recent article on Intel is from around a year ago, when I gave the company a neutral rating. Since then, INTC has moved mostly in line with the market. I last covered Nvidia in August, giving the company a neutral rating and noting its elevated valuation -- since then, NVDA has moved down by a couple of percentage points. With Intel and Nvidia announcing a huge deal on Thursday, it is time to take another look at these two companies.

What Happened?

On Thursday, two of the largest chip companies announced a major deal: Nvidia and Intel will work together when it comes to developing PC and data center chips in the future. On top of that, Nvidia will buy a $5 billion stake in Intel.

The market reacted very positively to this news, with Nvidia trading up by a couple of percentage points while INTC saw its shares soar by around 25% at the time of writing. Since Intel has a way lower market capitalization compared to its much more valuable peer, Nvidia, the smaller relative share price increase that Nvidia experienced still resulted in a bigger absolute increase in its market capitalization, at around $100 billion, versus an increase of around $30 billion for Intel. On the other hand, one can argue that INTC shareholders seem a lot more enthusiastic about this deal, as the share price jump for INTC is extraordinary and has lifted INTC's shares to a new yearly high, whereas NVDA trades below the highs seen this summer. I believe that the more pronounced enthusiasm among INTC shareholders makes sense -- combining with the leading player tech-wise should help Intel a lot, as INTC has fallen behind its competitors in several areas, especially data center chips.

The Equity Stake

The two parties agreed that NVDA will buy a $5 billion stake in INTC as part of the deal. This will happen at a price of around $23.30 per share, meaning Nvidia will buy around 215 million shares of Intel. While that naturally dilutes Intel's shareholders to some degree, I still think that this is a good development for Intel. Not only does this strengthen Intel's rather weak balance sheet -- Intel has a net debt position of around $29 billion -- but this also gives Nvidia an incentive to make the partnership work for both sides. After all, if Intel earns a lot of money via this new deal with Nvidia, Intel's share price should climb a lot, and Nvidia will profit from that thanks to its multi-billion-dollar stake. If, on the other hand, the deal wouldn't work out well for Intel, NVDA could lose some money via its investment in Intel. NVDA owning a portion of Intel thus makes sure that both companies have good reasons to make the deal work and have it be profitable for both parties.

For Nvidia, buying a $5 billion stake in Intel is easy -- NVDA has a strong balance sheet with a net cash position of around $46 billion, so it can easily afford to invest a couple of billion dollars in Intel -- on top of its investments in other companies, such as CoreWeave (CRWV).

The Cooperation Deal

The likely more important deal is the planned cooperation between Intel and Nvidia, although there are more uncertainties here, as we do not yet know how successful those ventures will be. Still, the market seems highly enthusiastic, and I believe that this is most likely some good news.

NVDA and INTC plan to develop a range of PC and data center products together, as reported here, which will include Nvidia-custom X86 CPUs for data centers as well as new SOCs that include Nvidia's RTX GPUs. These will all be manufactured by Intel, which makes sense, of course, as Nvidia operates with a fabless business model and does not have significant manufacturing capacity.

Intel has had a lot of execution problems in the past, but I believe that this deal suggests that Nvidia has trust in Intel's ability to execute in the future. If NVDA weren't trusting Intel, they most likely would not want to partner with them and have them manufacture these future products. This doesn't guarantee that there won't be any manufacturing problems, of course, but when a strong management team like Nvidia's decides that Intel is the right partner for this project, then that's a vote of confidence that shouldn't be ignored. This deal could thus be interpreted as a good sign that Intel will be able to perform better operationally going forward. Also, with Intel manufacturing these chips, its manufacturing business gets a nice boost, which I like as well.

The two companies also agreed to work on better integration between their respective tech, which will primarily happen via Nvidia's NVLink. According to the statement the two companies gave out, this will enable them to

Integrate the strengths of Nvidia’s AI and accelerated computing with Intel’s leading CPU technologies and x86 ecosystem to deliver cutting-edge solutions for customers.

While Intel seems like the bigger winner at first sight, I see some advantages for Nvidia as well. Via its stake in INTC, it will benefit when the latter is successful and the deal works out well, and Nvidia is likely also highly interested in becoming less reliant on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), primarily due to geopolitical risks. While INTC can't replace TSM anytime soon, the two working together on new chips and Intel being the manufacturing partner could eventually position INTC to take on more manufacturing tasks for Nvidia down the road -- not next quarter, but a couple of years from now. This would also fit well with the broader US strategy of becoming less reliant on China & Taiwan and improving supply chain resilience.

Are NVDA And INTC A Buy?

I recently covered Nvidia and gave it a Neutral rating, and I believe that NVDA remains a hold following this deal. NVDA has excellent tech and continues to grow at a nice pace, but its growth is slowing down to some degree, and at more than $4 trillion in market capitalization, near-term upside could be limited.

Intel looks extremely expensive when we look at this year's earnings per share estimate of just $0.11, but that's not very telling. Intel earned $5 per share and more not too long ago, and it could get back there if it successfully turns things around. The new deal with NVDA could boost its tech and the attractiveness of its portfolio, and it could be a sign that NVDA's management believes in a successful turnaround -- why else would they want to own billions worth of INTC stock? If INTC gets back to earning the profits it earned just a couple of years ago, it would be pretty cheap at the $30s level.

While I would not call Intel a low-risk stock -- things still can go wrong, and the balance sheet remains weak even following the NVDA deal -- INTC has significant upside if things go right. For a company to double its market capitalization from $140 billion should be easier than for a company to double its market capitalization from $4 trillion, all else equal. I thus rate NVDA a Hold and a buy on a potential pull-back, while I rate INTC a potential Buy for those who believe in its turnaround story.

data.title: Godfather Of AI Picks Up Intel: Is It Time To Put It Down? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This morning, news broke that Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is taking a big bet on Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC), announcing a $5B stake in the legacy semi company, plus introducing a joint plan to develop PC and data center chips.

Intel, which we’ve been screaming is a brainless buy under $20 per share this year, is up as much as 30% in pre-market on the news crossing $30 per share for the first time since last July, and Nvidia, which we doubled down on yesterday, is up over 2%. We think the positives of this deal are getting priced in, and it's time to take some profits. The fact that the foundry business was left out makes us believe Intel still has one more woe to resolve.

Hands in Intel now?

Intel is getting scooped up: the U.S. government has a 10% stake, announced in late August at $8.9B investment in common stock at $20.47 per share; SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) put in a $2B investment around mid-August; and now Nvidia joined the bunch with a $5B investment in Intel’s common stock at a purchase price of $23.28 per share. The chart below is a rough estimate of the current stakes in Intel.

For the greater part of this year, everyone hated on Intel, and now that narrative is changing. The stock price is reacting, too, which makes us believe we’re entering a risky zone because when it comes to Intel’s foundry business (it’s main woe) things are still in an awkward place.

Intel’s Foundry Services is not yet in either of the two case scenarios we see playing out yet: 1. Attracting external customers and ramping 18A and then 14A with High NA, or 2. Spinning off the foundry business and focusing on being a product company. At the Deutsche Bank conference last month, Intel told investors that it was in a “good cash position” (something we haven’t heard in the same sentence as Intel in a while) and that now it’s a matter of feeling out demand for its 14A node, which is the next-gen after 18A, to invest more capital. That signals more of what Intel CEO Li Bu Tan said on the Q2 earnings call and in the 10Q filing, which is that the company won’t spend until it can guarantee demand. Since then, we’ve seen a lot more players take a stake in the company, which is a big positive.

What would’ve been ideal, however, is to see Nvidia become an external customer for Intel’s foundry business, which has been struggling with high cash burn and no external customers. Nvidia didn’t go as far as to hitch its wagon to Intel’s chip-making side of the business and is sticking to TSMC (TSM). Still, Nvidia is hitching itself to Intel on other fronts, particularly data center and PC ones.

What this deal means for Intel’s product line

While this isn’t a game-changer for Intel’s foundry business yet, it is one for its product business. It means that Intel’s products can once again gain a competitive edge using Nvidia. As part of the deal, “The companies will focus on seamlessly connecting Nvidia and Intel architectures using Nvidia NVLink — integrating the strengths of Nvidia’s AI and accelerated computing with Intel’s leading CPU technologies and x86 ecosystem to deliver cutting-edge solutions for customers.” Intel will design Nvidia-custom x86 CPUs, which Nvidia will integrate into its AI infrastructure platforms.

Nvidia will also be providing Intel with custom graphics chips to package into its PC processors, providing Intel x86 with a much-needed edge against Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) on the client front. The following chart from Tom’s Hardware outlines the client x86 market share against AMD.

Intel will now “build and offer to the market x86 system-on-chips (SOCs) that integrate Nvidia RTX GPU chiplets. These new x86 RTX SOCs will power a wide range of PCs that demand integration of world-class CPUs and GPUs.” We don’t have an exact roadmap of what this collaboration will look like yet, but the phrase “multiple generations” was thrown out, which leads us to believe this is a catalyst that’ll stretch out.

Nvidia’s Jensen Huang noted the following about the deal:

This historic collaboration tightly couples Nvidia’s AI and accelerated computing stack with Intel’s CPUs and the vast x86 ecosystem — a fusion of two world-class platforms. Together, we will expand our ecosystems and lay the foundation for the next era of computing.

And, Tan added that:

Intel’s leading data center and client computing platforms, combined with our process technology, manufacturing, and advanced packaging capabilities, will complement Nvidia’s AI and accelerated computing leadership to enable new breakthroughs for the industry.

Intel’s rivals, both TSMC and AMD, are trading down in pre-market on this morning's news, as shown below.

The concern for TSMC is that Intel’s foundry business could actually grow legs and Nvidia could dabble in entertaining some orders, in which case TSMC would be forced to buy the High NA tool (which is around €400M) from ASML (ASML). For AMD, the situation is messier because Intel's cozying up to Nvidia gives Intel’s products a competitive edge that’ll push back AMD’s relentless share gain in the CPU market this year. Last month, Bank of America reported that AMD and Arm (ARM) share gains against Intel continued during 2QCY25. The note stated that AMD posted “strong CPU revenue share gain… up +120bps QoQ to 29.5%, and extending its +300bps gain in CY24,” while ARM is “gaining strongly in servers.” Intel has been slashing prices to stay competitive, but we’re expecting potential price increases after this deal, which should benefit Intel’s financials massively next year.

Stock price performance & Valuation

Intel’s YTD chart is nothing short of volatile. The stock dipped below $20 per share eight times this year before recovering to above $22 each time. This bounce is different because it pushes Intel stock past $30 per share for the first time since July 2024 (over a year ago). We think the positive of the deal are priced in for the near-term at current levels.

Even without counting the pre-market gains, Intel is up almost 24% YTD, outperforming the S&P 500 (SPY), which is up 13% during the same period.

Intel is still cheap, although not as cheap as it used to be. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 211.36x against a group average of 25x. On an EV/Sales basis, the stock is trading at a forward EV/Sales of 2.94x.

Our belief is that this deal means a lot more for Intel than it does for Nvidia; a $5B stake is not a big deal for Nvidia, which sits on a $4.14T market cap, but it is a huge deal for Intel. It’s a green sign that Intel is leaving its sub-$95B market-cap behind and regaining a competitive position for its x86.

We think it's a good day to take profits, considering we still have a big fat question mark on the foundry side of the business, which is really the chip on Intel's shoulder now, more so now that the deal with Nvidia allows the company to regain its competitive edge within the CPU market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AWF: Stable Fixed Income For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
The AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund (NYSE:AWF) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with income by investing in fixed income securities. AWF pays out an appealing distribution rate of $0.80/share, yielding 7.09% on a forward basis.

About AllianceBernstein Global High Income Fund

AWF was launched on July 28, 1993, by AllianceBernstein as a diversified fixed-income strategy. AWF has roughly $994 million in net assets, with an average of $2.16 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis. AWF is actively managed, adjusting risk throughout the economic cycle; AWF is meant to be held for a long time horizon as an income-oriented investment strategy.

AWF employs a quantitative and fundamental approach to developing the bond portfolio. The strategy invests in both domestic and international bond issuances, inclusive of government and corporate bonds. Issuances within the strategy may be denominated in U.S. dollars, oftentimes called dollar bonds, as well as foreign currencies. As of August 2025, the strategy was primarily invested in USD-denominated issuances.

AWF is broadly diversified across 1,207 individual issuances, with the top 10 holdings making up only 4.8% of the total portfolio weight. AWF is largely diversified across international regions, with U.S. exposure accounting for 66% of the portfolio weight. The strategy is largely diversified across the EU, UK, and Latin America, with some exposure to APAC.

The strategy is largely invested in high-yield bonds, or bonds that are below investment grade. BB-rated bonds make up the majority of exposure, with a portfolio weight of 43.81%. In total, high-yield corporate bonds make up 74.67% of the portfolio, with investment grade accounting for 13.47%.

From a value perspective, AWF has an effective duration of 2.86 years, making the strategy modestly sensitive to interest rate policy. For reference, a higher duration rate indicates more sensitivity to rate changes.

On Sept. 17, 2025, the U.S. Federal Reserve cut the federal funds rate by 25bps to a target range of 4%-4.25%. Accordingly, the Fed is expecting to lower rates by another 50bps by the end of 2025, with an additional 25bps cut in 2026. The rate cut can be beneficial to foreign issuances as the USD may weaken relative to foreign currencies. This can be particularly beneficial to dollar bonds, or foreign issuances denominated in USD. Holding all else equal, foreign dollar bonds should theoretically become cheaper for the foreign issuers if the USD were to weaken relative to the country’s currency, easing exchange rates and making the issuances more serviceable.

Treasury yields were modestly impacted following the rate cut announcement, likely due to the cut being highly anticipated going into the cut. With rate policy being more or less set for the remainder of the year, I do not anticipate substantial movements that will impact bond yields.

One factor that may impact international bonds is U.S. tariff policy. So far, tariffs haven’t hindered growth across the European Bloc. The Wall Street Journal reported that industrial production in the eurozone turned to growth in July 2025. Some suspect that consumer goods in the EU may have been impacted by some front-running earlier in the year, potentially leading to productivity softening later in 2025. One factor that the report noted was the turn in Germany’s fiscal policy towards loosening fiscal rules to boost spending, as referenced by Reuters in March 2025. Accordingly, looser fiscal policy is expected to create “a €500b special fund to boost the country’s infrastructure.” This investment initiative could potentially offset any headwinds that may result from U.S. tariff policies, benefiting local industries.

Overall, I believe AWF may be well-positioned going forward, potentially receiving tailwinds from the shift in U.S. rate policy.

From a fund-level perspective, AWF can be an appealing fixed-income strategy for investors seeking domestic and international, high-yield exposure. The strategy has the flexibility to rotate assets without limit across regions, duration, issuer type, and ratings. This can be appealing to investors that may not want to actively manage their portfolio’s bond exposure.

AWF pays out a monthly distribution rate annualized at $0.80/share, yielding 7.09% on a forward-looking basis. Distributions have been relatively consistent over the years, providing investors with some predictability for financial planning purposes.

AWF is one of the lower-cost fixed-income CEFs on the market with an expense ratio of 100bps. AWF commands a much larger portfolio relative to peers with $994 million in net assets, providing investors with a certain degree of liquidity for trading and rebalancing purposes.

Looking at performance, AWF has historically performed at the midpoint of its peers, though the fund has outperformed looking back at 6-month performance on a total return basis.

Distributions are also relatively moderate relative to peer CEFs. One peer fund, the PGIM Global High Yield Fund (GHY), may provide an alternative for AWF given its strong distribution yield and historical performance. GHY is relatively smaller with $542 million in net assets, though the strategy maintains similar diversification across 875 holdings. GHY pays out a higher distribution rate of $1.26/share, yielding 9.51% on a forward-looking basis.

Risks Related to AWF

AWF is an actively managed, fixed-income strategy with international exposure, providing certain risks to investors, including manager risk, interest rate risk, inflation risk, and currency risk, among others. Given the international exposure, the fund may be exposed to foreign central bank rate policies as well as U.S. rate policy that may influence exchange rates, potentially impacting cash flows to a certain extent. The strategy will largely be dependent on the portfolio management team’s expertise in security selection and tactical positioning.

Final Thoughts

AWF is an appealing fixed-income CEF that can provide investors with current income as a long-term investment. The strategy may be well-positioned for further interest rate cuts in the United States, potentially leading to improved performance going forward. I am recommending AWF with a "Buy" rating; however, investors may consider GHY as an alternative to AWF.

data.title: Growth Ahead For UMH And Flagship In Manufactured Housing
data.description:
Manufactured housing is well positioned with a price point that affords a large surplus for both producer and consumer. The supply of MH has been artificially held down by onerous financial and zoning regulations, which has created a substantial undersupply. This undersupply is beneficial to the incumbents who have the capability to get through the red tape and can sell into proportionally higher demand.

We believe the MH sector will generally outperform, and we see Flagship (OTCPK:MHCUF) and UMH Properties (NYSE:UMH) as the best positioned within it. Let us begin with the fundamental and regulatory factors pointing toward sector level success and then move on to the individual company analysis.

Sector fundamentals

There are multiple concepts that we believe favor forward growth in MH:

  • A low percentage of MH in the overall housing market is a big part of the affordable housing crisis, and the growing concerns are an impetus for loosening regulation.
  • Regulation is loosening federally.
  • Regulation is loosening at the state and local level.
  • A large and growing price disparity between MH and site-built creates a surplus to be split between buyer and supplier.
  • Better financing options will stimulate demand.

Company-level analysis

At the company level, we favor UMH and MHCUF for the following reasons:

  • Geographic advantage in top MH zones
  • Valuation
  • Same-store NOI superiority
  • MH Pure-plays
  • Manufacturers upping supply improves the profit capture rate for community owners.

Let’s dive right in.

MH is a small percentage of housing compared to history

Shipments of manufactured housing have increased significantly in the last 10 years.

However, the volume is still quite low compared to history. In fact, the last 20 years have been the lowest MH building volume in the last 70 years.

At today’s pace of MH shipments, they make up about 10% of the single-family housing market. Looking at the number of site-built housing starts below, we can see that MH used to be as much as 30% of the housing market back in the 70s.

Regulation on MH got really tight both in terms of zoning and financing, which has been a substantial deterrent to volume in the last 20 years.

I posit that such restrictions on the cheapest form of permanent housing have been a massive contributor to the affordable housing crisis. When the percentage of MH in housing moves from 30% to 10%, that is undoubtedly going to have implications for general affordability.

There are articles everywhere about how difficult it is for young people to afford their first home. The average age of first-time homebuyers has gone up considerably, and even for those who can afford it, the cost is so substantial that it is not the wealth-building proposition that homeownership used to be.

Given the crisis, there is growing social and therefore political pressure to do something about affordable housing. Manufactured housing is increasingly seen as the solution.

At a federal level, there are 2 substantial changes that improve the prospects for MH volume:

  1. 100% bonus depreciation on MH as per the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA)
  2. Loosening financing restrictions, making it easier for homebuyers of MH to get FHA loans. Specifically, they have broadened the scope of what qualifies.

States are also loosening MH regulation. The states mentioned in the link are likely just the tip of the iceberg, with general social and political will encouraging other states to follow suit.

The impetus for such a push for manufactured housing is the sheer magnitude of price disparity. MH can still be bought for an average of around $120K, while site-built homes have gotten relatively more expensive, at an average approaching $370K.

Source: Construction Coverage

That $250K gap in price point provides a lot of room for creativity in finding solutions that work for both the consumer and the provider of MH.

Builders are increasingly experimenting with larger and nicer manufactured housing, such as two-story buildings and multitenant units. These larger buildings now qualify for HUD due to recent changes from HUD Secretary Scott Turner.

With higher quality MH available, home buyers do not have to take as much of a sacrifice on living conditions while still getting a massive discount on price point.

The quality of the product can work in tandem with more available financing options to substantially drive demand for MH.

It has previously been difficult to qualify for MH loans, which meant buyers had to have very high down payments relative to property value. The ratio was such that the down payment required for MH was close to that required for site-built homes since site-built homes allow for higher loan-to-value ratios.

With the increasing availability of FHA loans for MH, MH is becoming substantially more affordable for buyers in overall price tag, monthly payment, and down payment. We think MH can capture a significant portion of would-be first-time buyers who have been priced out of the site-built housing market.

For all of the above reasons, we think manufactured housing will be a rapidly growing sector. Let us now turn our attention to which entities within the sector will capture the lion’s share of the profits.

Sector participants

There are 3 big categories of MH industry participants:

  • MH Manufacturers
  • MH financiers (loans and insurance)
  • MH community owners

If we are correct about the overall volume increase of manufactured housing deliveries, each of these industry participants will get a nice revenue boost. Profit sharing between the participants will depend on strategic positioning and relative scarcity.

I posit that community owners will get the better end of the deal.

There is still relatively little land available that is zoned for MH and properly amenitized. This makes community owners the bottleneck and therefore the segment capable of enforcing pricing power.

In contrast, the manufacturing can be scaled up to meet demand, albeit with some delay.

William Boor (CEO of Cavco) noted on the 2Q earnings call (their fiscal Q1) that they are significantly increasing manufacturing volume:

“Over the last several quarters, we've been executing on a plan to push production up where we have the backlog to support it”

Cavco is positioned to perform reasonably well. I suspect margins will stay roughly flat while volume increases for overall proportional earnings growth.

Financiers can also expand volume to meet demand. There have even been new entrants into MH financing, with the MH REITs stepping in to offer financing to their tenants. As such, I suspect MH loans will remain at normal economic margins for an okay return to the companies that offer them.

The bottleneck seems to be properly zoned land in well-managed MH communities. As such, I think the community owners are positioned to capture an outsized share of profits as the sector grows.

To some extent it is already happening, with the REITs showing a capacity to raise rents at a pace that substantially exceeds inflation or GDP.

Same-store NOI growth across the MH REIT sector has been very strong. Equity LifeStyle’s (ELS) SSNOI is charted below.

The other large-cap manufactured housing REIT is Sun Communities (SUI) with its SSNOI charted below.

Each has been averaging about 5% same-store NOI growth for the last decade.

Their strong MH growth is being partially offset by their other businesses, such as RVs and marinas.

If we look instead at the pure-play MH REITs, the growth is closer to 10% annually.

UMH Properties has roughly doubled the same-store NOI growth of its larger peers.

Small-cap, Canadian-domiciled Flagship Communities has not been around as long but is clocking in at 12% average same-store NOI growth.

The pure-play MH REITs are significantly outperforming the larger mixed-asset MH REITs. Despite the growth advantages, the pure plays are actually trading at a large discount to peers.

Valuation

ELS and SUI trade at 22.8X and 21.2X 2025 AFFO, respectively. UMH is available at 15.9X AFFO.

Flagship does not populate on the screener because of its Canadian domicile, but its properties and management are all in the U.S., so it is absolutely a fundamental peer. It is trading at 13.61X AFFO.

In my opinion, the cheaper valuation and higher growth of UMH and MHCUF make them clearly better than SUI or ELS.

Additionally, I think the geographic footprints of UMH and MHCUF are advantaged.

Geographic advantage

These smaller REITs operate in the states that are most attuned to MH.

Flagship’s property map radiates around its Kentucky headquarters.

It so happens that Kentucky is the number 2 state in MH concentration, with fully 31.6% of its single-family housing consisting of MH compared to the national average of about 10%.

UMH’s property map is also concentrated in the eastern and middle USA, which is where most of the darker shaded states on the map above are.

These areas tend to be a bit friendlier to MH development than the coastal regions, which has allowed the REITs to get their land zoned more efficiently.

UMH in particular has a very large bank of land that is already zoned and, in many cases, adjacent to existing communities. This will allow them to build thousands of additional sites at minimal incremental land cost.

Bull thesis summary

UMH and Flagship have among the best organic growth rates in real estate, yet are trading at discounted valuations. We see the growth continuing for the foreseeable future due to the aforementioned macro and regulatory factors favoring the manufactured housing sector.

Since indexes primarily go by market cap, these companies are virtually nonexistent in any real estate ETFs. As such, real estate investors are woefully underexposed to what is and has been the fastest-growing real estate sector. We are substantially overweight UMH and MHCUF.

Risks to thesis

Aside from the normal equity investing risks, I think the most relevant risk to this article is the potential for the regulatory pendulum to swing too far.

A little bit of red tape loosening is great for the industry. If it gets too loose, however, it could introduce an oversupply threat. Part of the ample pricing power of MH communities is the scarcity of high-quality, safe, and well-amenitized MH communities. If the red tape gets removed to a point where it becomes fast and easy to set up a new MH community, it opens the door for some very well-capitalized players to flood the market.

I think regulation overall is still fairly tight, so I am not worried about this today, but it is a factor to watch.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SIXG: Strong Buy In The Connectivity Sector
data.description:
Introduction

The Defiance Connective Technologies ETF (NASDAQ:SIXG) aims to invest funds into the next generation of connection and telecommunications hardware and software, especially those that have use cases in AI and advanced computing. The fund looks to invest in companies that are powering the next computing revolution, who need the best and fastest connections to ensure their products and platforms operate at the highest efficiency. Please see below for some key facts of the fund, taken from the prospectus.

Their top holdings are as follows.

We are overall very bullish on the investment thesis of this ETF, as we believe that enhanced telecommunications hardware and software will be incredibly important in this new age of computing. In the following section, we will review some high level trends and news that further our bullishness in the sector.

Industry Trends and News

The 6G wireless market is projected to grow rapidly

The next generation of wireless connective technology is 6G, which replaces the current standard of 5G. Estimates show that the overall market for this technology is forecasted to grow exponentially over the next decade, per Root Analysis.

We believe that the majority of this shift and growth will be driven by the needs of all of these companies and governments who are building the latest and greatest AI models and networks - these require significant amounts of connective power to ensure the models run smoothly and efficiently from a power basis. We believe that this overall trend will still continue, and that as antiquated 4G and 5G networks become more and more obsolete, the 6G market will turn from a cutting-edge use case to a mainstream product - and that will fuel additional growth in the market. This is a powerful factor that would spur investments into the companies in this ETF.

AI investments are growing globally

The overall AI infrastructure market and overarching investment theme that has dominated the markets are set to continue further, as a recent report shows that Microsoft, Nvidia, and other tech giants are planning a massive $40B investment into AI related enterprises in the United Kingdom. This multi-corporate investment affair is also coming side by side with the Trump administration’s visit to the UK, and if prior international travel for Trump is any insight, it could mean that more deals are to come. We believe that this is positive for a few reasons. Firstly, it puts out the fire that has been brewing regarding the ‘AI Bubble’ for the time being - this shows that investment is very much still ongoing and investors are not done putting money into AI-related companies. Secondly, this shows that the governments around the world are still investing in building out their AI portfolios and positioning, and this is another large corner of the market that should bode well for firms in this ETF. Ultimately, we view these continued investments in the AI space as very bullish for this ETF.

Data centers are being built out consistently, for AI and non-AI use cases

Many of the top names in this fund are suppliers of chips, networking gear, cables, connection points, energy, and server racks for data centers - both in the edge computing and AI markets as well as other computing needs for various clients. Overall, we are seeing trends that show data center growth and investment are not stopping - and this is beneficial for the names in the fund. For example, BlackRock announced an investment of $678M in the UK for data centers, Google revealed a $5B investment in the UK for data center related activities, and the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia is exploring partnerships with private capital firms to fund its massive data center expansion efforts. These instances of investment into the infrastructure needed for the computing of tomorrow are all signs that show these trends are not stopping, and that there is consistent money flowing into this sector - which is fantastic for the names in SIXG. We believe that these trends will continue for the foreseeable future, and with a rate cut on the horizon, borrowing money to fund expansion efforts will only get cheaper. This is a very bullish sign for the companies in this ETF.

Peer Comparison

As we discussed, we are very bullish on the outlook of 5G/6G technologies and telecom growth in general. However, that does not mean that SIXG is the best way to invest in these trends. We will look at SIXG against other ETFs that have a similar theme or holdings:

  • Pacer Data and Digital Revolution ETF (TRFK)

  • SPDR S&P Telecom ETF (XTL)

  • iShares U.S. Telecommunications ETF (IYZ)

We acknowledge that these ETFs do not exactly follow the theme of SIXT, but they have overlap in the holdings and/or macro factors that impact these ETFs. TRFK is the ETF that is most similar to SIXG in terms of holdings with 34.6% overlap with SIXG.

First we look at performance:

We see that in the past 3 years, there is quite a difference in the performance of the 4 ETFs. It is hard to visually see the difference in the chart, so we pulled the numbers out into a table:

ETF

3 Year Performance

Diff From Previous

IYZ

34.96%

XTL

68.98%

97%

SIXG

96.95%

41%

TRFK

194.36%

100%

We see that as we go from the lowest-performing to the highest-performing ETF, there are large gaps in performance, with at least a 41% gap between each one. Of course, TRFK stands out from the pack, as is also evident in the chart. SIXG is in 2nd place with almost 100% gain in the last 3 years.

Next we look at the expense ratios as reported by Seeking Alpha:

We see that the 4 ETFs generally have similar expense ratios, but TRFK stands out again as the highest one and with the largest difference from the next highest. SIXG has the lowest expense.

We now look at the 4-year average dividend yields of the ETFs:

There is a wider gap in yields for the 4 ETFs than we saw with expense ratios. IYZ wins this one with 2.39%, and SIXG is in 2nd place with 1.32%. TRFK does not have a dividend.

Overall, this comparison looks good for SIXG. It is in 1st or 2nd place across all of the metrics we looked at, and we especially like the (relatively) low expense ratio. The high performance over the past 3 years is promising for the telecommunications strategy as well. One concern could be that they have one of the best price performances of the group, so they may be overvalued. However, as we discussed, there look to be a lot of additional growth opportunities in the sector, so we are not concerned about the fund’s ability to perform in the next few years.

Risks to the thesis

AI Investment trends stop

There is a possibility that the AI investment cycle dries up, or investors rotate away from the AI trade. This would be highly detrimental to this ETF - but as we have shown above, from all the news and trends that we are analyzing and tracking, it seems that the opposite is the case. AI investments are still happening across the globe, and forecasts estimate more and more capital deployment into the sector over the next decade as the future of computing gets funded and built out.

Low Trading Volume

In the past 3 months, SIXG has had an average daily volume of 61.16k which is generally considered to be illiquid. This means that if you were to buy the fund, it may be difficult to sell your shares in the future at a good price. One positive side is that the fund’s value comes primarily from its holdings which are generally large, very liquid companies, so the risk of a bad valuation of your shares is lower. However, keep in mind that it may take longer than expected to close any position in SIXG due to its low volume.

Conclusion

To wrap up, we believe in the overall goal of this ETF. The names that this fund holds are also leaders in the AI, advanced computing, telecommunications, and connectivity spaces. They all make exceptional products that are critical to the AI revolution that is happening currently. We view this overall market and industry as a very strong investment opportunity over the next decade. This fund should do well as the overall industry grows, invests more, and scales expansion efforts further.

Furthermore, we see that in comparison to similar funds, SIXG looks quite attractive. It has the lowest expense ratio of the funds we looked at and one of the best performances and dividend yields. We see that not only is the 5G/6G telecommunications strategy effective (and we believe it will continue to be effective in the near future), but also the SIXG stands out from other ETFs employing a similar strategy.

As such, we rate SIXG as a strong buy.

data.title: The Stage Is Set For A Sizeable Equity Market Pullback
data.description:
The stage is set for a potentially sizeable equity market pullback.

Friday will be quarterly opex, and that should help to release the stock market from its current malaise of tight daily trading ranges. This has resulted in realized volatility levels falling to very low levels, with even three-month realized volatility at levels not seen since July 2024.

On top of all this, liquidity levels have fallen sharply, with the treasury general account now back to $850 billion. It has likely resulted in reserve balances held at the Fed falling to around the $3 trillion level, although the number will need to be confirmed following the release of the weekly SOMA report after the close of trading on Thursday, September 18.

When combining the options expiration with declining levels of liquidity in the overall market, it could create a window for not only market volatility to develop but also a case for a sizeable drawdown in the overall stock market.

Gamma Release

Gamma levels in the S&P 500 (SP500) are quite elevated, and that has caused the S&P 500 to pin around the 6,600 strike price. However, once we pass Friday, gamma levels in the index should fall, resulting in the market starting to move more freely.

When the market is in positive gamma, it means that market makers are buyers of the index when it falls and sellers of the index when it rises. Gamma levels are highest right now at 6,600, with overall gamma levels at almost $52 billion per 1% move. However, after Friday, that gamma amount will drop to just $32 billion, a 1% move, and that means the pinning effect at 6,600 will not be as great. Unfortunately, it doesn't tell us which way the market could move, because it could move up just as easily as it could move down.

Even though volatility risk is asymmetric, we know that any move higher in the index will have to come as a grind, given that realized volatility is so low. Currently, 9-day vol is at 5.3, 21-day vol is at 8.6, and 63-day vol is at 8.9. When applying the rule of 16 to these values, it means that anytime the S&P 500 moves above 33 bps, the 9-day realized volatility will start rising, and that sends the entire volatility complex into motion, meaning that implied volatility starts to rise. We know that once implied volatility starts rising, it tends to lead to a pullback in the market.

Liquidity

At the same time, we have seen the Treasury General Account reach $850 billion as the reverse repo facility has fallen to below $20 billion. That would mean that overall reserve balances are likely around $3 trillion based on estimates, versus last week's value of around $3.15 trillion.

To this point, there has been no fallout to risk assets from the decline in reserve balances, but that may still be coming, especially if there is a lag in the time it takes to transmit the changes in reserves to the changes in factors like margin and leverage.

With options expiration on September 19 and liquidity levels greatly reduced, it sets up the potential for increasing volatility and a sizeable equity market pullback.

data.title: RLX Technology: Turning Positive On M&A And New Products (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become bullish on RLX Technology (NYSE:RLX). It has taken steps to realize its growth potential in new geographical markets and product segments.

My prior August 21, 2024, article touched on the mixed read-throughs from RLX's 2Q24 disclosures.

Positive On Inorganic Expansion

At its 2Q25 briefing on Aug. 22, RLX highlighted the "consolidation of our recently acquired European e-vapor company in June." My take is that this is a wise move. Its new subsidiary is "UK vape retailer Totally Wicked," or "TW," according to ECigIntelligence's research.

Ispire (ISPR) mentioned at its May '25 analyst call that "the UK led the European market by banning (e-cigarette) disposables." The expectation is that the rest of "EU would follow suit in due course," as per ISPR's comments.

TW's addition ensures that RLX remains relevant in an evolving regulatory environment.

The firm acknowledged at the second-quarter results meeting that "disposable products have historically been an integral part of our portfolio." But it noted that "we recognize that the future lies in more sustainable solutions." Its new daughter company's "full suite of capabilities and services in the local market" was also emphasized.

TW boasts a wide assortment of e-cigarettes, including vape kits that come with rechargeable batteries (to substitute for disposables). In my opinion, the acquiree will aid RLX's product mix optimization efforts. Separately, the United Kingdom can be the group's new growth engine.

Euromonitor published a report (not publicly available) titled "Smokeless Tobacco, E-Vapour Products and Heated Tobacco in the United Kingdom" in June 2025. TW's share of the sizeable $4 billion UK vape market is a mid-single-digit percent, as per this research organization's estimates.

RLX began venturing outside its home market, the PRC, in '23.

Non-Chinese operations only contributed slightly over a quarter of its top line last year. There's room for the enterprise to increase its overseas presence.

In the past, RLX's U.K. e-cigs were mainly sold via e-commerce sites or wholesalers.

TW has a network of 100+ physical outlets. The additional distribution channel is likely to translate into higher sales in this country. I also think it's a big difference to benefit from on-the-ground learnings regarding smokers' preferences.

RLX is exploring the possibility of entering "additional European countries" next year based on management's remarks at the Q2 call. The newly acquired UK business should act as a launchpad for its ambitions in this continent.

Potential Product Launches Have Huge Promise

The company is diversifying beyond e-vapor. It revealed at the late-Aug. earnings call that the "2B (To-Business) prototypes" for its "modern oral nicotine products (ONPs)" are done. The next step is introducing these items to "the 2C (To-Consumer) market." RLX's proposal could substantially widen its "Total Addressable Market," or "TAM."

UBS Group (UBS) issued a research note (not accessible in public) with the title "Global Tobacco: US Nicotine Survey" dated Sept. 5, 2025. The bank forecasts that sales of "nicotine pouches," a common form of oral nicotine, will rise +122% in the next five years to $10 billion in the United States by the end of this decade; this category is also predicted to account for a high-teens percentage of American cigarette consumption in volume terms.

My view is that these products should have favorable prospects around the world, because they cater to the needs of specific user segments.

RLX's Q2 analyst call commentary stressed that "oral nicotine fits seamlessly into the lifestyle of office workers and frequent travelers."

Most people have the experience of struggling to squeeze everything into suitcases as they pack for trips. You would assume that smokers prefer sachet-like nicotine pouches over vaping devices and related refillables in that kind of scenario.

On the other hand, white-collar workers have to be mindful of how they are judged by their bosses and colleagues, who might be non-smokers. ONPs can be easily consumed at the workstation, as they are smoke-free and don't emit any odors.

Also, "nicotine pouch users are (on average) 10 years younger than cigarette users," as indicated by UBS's early-September publication cited above. The financial institution's analysis found that these are perceived to be "less harmful nicotine products" by this demographic due to the absence of tobacco.

I see RLX's growth runway being extended with its entry into the ONP space.

You Should Consider These Variant Views

There may be post-M&A integration issues relating to the "Totally Wicked" deal. If RLX doesn't work well with TW's management, its future UK market performance might fall short of expectations.

RLX could take a longer-than-anticipated period of time to market its new ONP-related items. It is also possible that people choose to stay with tried-and-tested oral nicotine brands.

My Closing Thoughts

RLX's rating is revised from a "Hold" to a "Buy."

The group's outlook has become better, considering its recent acquisition and new product roadmap.

My fair price-to-sales valuation for RLX is 7.7 times by using its domestic peer Smoore (OTCPK:SMORF) (6969.HK) as a benchmark. This translates into a 48% (7.7/5.2) upside, taking into account the stock's current 5.2x P/S.
